No Poverty.reason: No direct or indirect evidence in the course syllabus links CAD skills or course content to poverty eradication.

Zero Hunger.reason: The course content on computer-aided design does not provide any direct or indirect evidence related to ending hunger or improving food security.

Good Health and Well-being.reason: There is no explicit or inferred connection between the CAD course content and health outcomes or well-being.

Quality Education.reason: The course directly provides quality technical and vocational skills (Target 4.4) through proficiency in CAD, developing a versatile and transferable skill set for various industries.
evidence: develop proficiency in a professional 2D and 3D CAD system
Develop a versatile, transferable skill set

Gender Equality.reason: The course syllabus does not contain any specific information or initiatives related to promoting gender equality.

Clean Water and Sanitation.reason: CAD skills for creating 'engineering plans' can be indirectly applied to designing water and sanitation infrastructure, providing a minimal link.

Affordable and Clean Energy.reason: The ability to 'simulate and analyze design performance' in CAD can indirectly contribute to improving energy efficiency in various designs and systems (Target 7.3).

Decent Work and Economic Growth.reason: The course explicitly provides a 'versatile, transferable skill set' applicable in 'planning, architecture, engineering, manufacturing,' directly supporting productive employment and innovation (Targets 8.2, 8.5).
evidence: Develop a versatile, transferable skill set
applicable in planning, architecture, engineering, manufacturing

Industry, Innovation and Infrastructure.reason: CAD is fundamental for developing 'complex objects and systems,' 'engineering plans,' '3D printing,' and 'manufacturing,' directly supporting resilient infrastructure, industrialization, and innovation (Targets 9.1, 9.4, 9.5).
evidence: Preparing designs for 3D printing and manufacturing
digital tools have revolutionized how we conceptualize, develop, and refine complex objects

Reduced Inequalities.reason: The course's aim to be 'welcoming and approachable for students from all academic backgrounds' provides a minimal, indirect link to promoting social inclusion (Target 10.2).
evidence: welcoming and approachable for students from all academic backgrounds

Sustainable Cities and Communities.reason: CAD skills are explicitly applicable in 'planning, architecture,' and creating 'maps, construction and engineering plans,' directly supporting inclusive and sustainable urbanization and human settlement planning (Target 11.3).

Responsible Consumption and Production.reason: The course teaches students to 'simulate and analyze design performance before physical production' and 'rapidly iterate and optimize designs,' directly promoting resource efficiency and waste reduction (Targets 12.2, 12.5).
evidence: Simulate and analyze design performance before physical production

Climate Action.reason: The ability to 'simulate and analyze design performance' in CAD can indirectly contribute to designing climate-resilient infrastructure, supporting climate adaptation (Target 13.1).

Life Below Water.reason: No direct or indirect evidence in the course syllabus relates to marine ecosystems or life below water.

Life on Land.reason: The course's focus on creating 'maps' and its affiliation with the 'Land Economics' department provides an indirect link to land use planning and ecosystem integration (Target 15.9).

Peace, Justice and Strong Institutions.reason: The course content does not provide any direct or indirect evidence related to peace, justice, or institutional strength.

Partnerships for the Goals.reason: No evidence in the course syllabus indicates a focus on partnerships for sustainable development.
